speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. I'm interviewing Rachel Hanan Hougnou. I am Lorrin Brasg. We are in Johannesburg, South Africa. This interview is being conducted in English. And this is tape seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we stay in-- in-- in-- in-- Belgium, we have to stay there for a-- a month. Because our vaccination has to shown positive. Otherwise we are not allowed in the Congo. I went in the Congo, and my sister together. We arrive at the Congo in December, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went right by plane. Because our brother, you know, have sent enough fund, and the tickets. And while we were in Brussels, we were in a nice place and everything. And we went, you know, to the Belgian Congo. And we arrive there after you know, my-- my brother-- my sister was engage to the-- you know, to the-- to-- to her fiance, for a day in Rhodes Island. Because they were, you know, like, boyfriends. So the day he was leaving Rhodes Island in 1938, he asked for his-- for hand in marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which my father agree. Because he say, I go to Africa, and I send her, you know, to-- for her, straight away, as soon as I make a little bit of money. I'll send for her tickets and whatever, and we'll get married. In the meantime, we arrive. It was nearly '39, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the war, you know, by the time he was making the paper ready for her to come, the paper-- you know, the war started in 1940. And no boats were allowed in Rhodes Island, nothing. You know, as we know very well, nobody was allowed to go from one place to another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived to the Congo, you know, and-- and we arrive. And my sister thought-- my sister thought well, surely a man cannot wait eight years to get his fiance. Though, when we were in Italy, we started-- you know, we receive letters and money from my brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was regularly-- regularly sending to my sister as well, gifts. But she thought maybe, you know, we couldn't get any letter. Surely you know, he feel sorry for her. He was her fiance. And maybe, you know, he just tried to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as soon as we arrive, you know, to the Belgian Congo, the next week he was staying in Stanleyville it's another-- another town of-- we arrived in Elizabethville, you know, the capital. He came, you know, straight away, took the plane.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he took the ring and he said, this time I'm not let you go until I get married. And I said, it's OK with me. And eventually-- he was faithful to her all these years of the war. He never took out any other woman, never got engaged, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- after a month, he bought everything. With my brothers together, we arranged for the shawl, for the dress, beautiful. And I become a bridesmaid. And my-- my other friend from the concentration camp, and we made a beautiful, lovely, big wedding. And he took his bride, and they went to live in So myself, I stay in the Elizabethville one month, two months, three months. And I was single. I was by myself. Eventually, I decide, you see, let me go and work. Because I was also-- during before the war, I was working in an office, like a bookkeeper, you know? You know, helping the-- helping-- help a bookkeeper, not exactly qualified. So I went there and I ask for the job to a firm, for a Jewish firm, which they agreed to give me. And I was working. And I was very, very well, you know, very well established.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, my brother went-- he moved to another place. And I was not staying with my brother anymore. But my firm was providing me a-- a lodging in the best hotel in town, in Elizabethville. And I worked for that firm in the Belgian Congo for 5 and a 1/2 years. I was very happy. I was having tremendous wages. And I was very happy. And I was not-- I didn't find nobody who wanted to marry me, I suppose. [LAUGHS] And you know, I was staying. And I was staying there for five, six years. And then you know, I decide to come to-- to-- to South of Rhodesia at the time, for a holiday. A friend of mine was come, said, let's go to have a holiday. I went to-- to-- to-- from Belgian Congo to Salisbury. At the time, it was called Salisbury. It was Rhodesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: After a week I was there, I met my husband, you know, my husband. Which he was also from Rhodes Island. But he never went to the concentration camp, you know? He was there previous, you know, one of the last-- he came here very young. And he was working with his brother. And he was comfortable, you know? And he wants to get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he proposed to me. And we got married into a few months. And then, I-- I went back again to the Belgian Congo, to finish with my boss. I took all my you know, belonging, or whatever. And I come back, and I got married in Zimbabwe. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25th January, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon, L-E-O-N, Hanan, H-A-N-A-N. And at the time-- it-- it-- and it happened to be very-- I mean, a very happy  marriage. And he was-- he's a-- a nice man, and he look after me. I never work anymore. And we had two-- two sons, that we educated very well. One is a lawyer, and one is a pharmacist. They are all in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Johannesburg now. They are married. One is Morris, M-O-R-R-I-S, Hanan, and he's the eldest. And the other-- and he's-- he's born in 1953, October 24. And the other one is Aaron Ronald. He is a lawyer. I tell you, he's in Johannesburg. And he works for a nice firm here. And he is-- he is-- he is born in-- in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they did they come to Johannesburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they left-- you know, and at the time, when they were studying, they-- they didn't have-- the-- in Zimbabwe-- no, at the time it was Rhodesia. They didn't have any university, not for law. Now, I think there, they have. Because there was a university, but there were only some-- were some faculties. They didn't have, at the time, not for law, not for pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they both went to Grahamstown. Because there you can finish all your high school, go-- your [INAUDIBLE], whatever. And you have to go to university if you want to go, to continue your study. And I was trying to help them in the homework and everything. Because whatever I didn't achieve myself, I wanted them to achieve, you know? To-- to-- to go to university, which I didn't go, which that was my aim in life. So they came, you know, to-- again to-- to Grahamstown, both of them. They both graduated in the same night. One pharmacist, one-- one LLB. But my one LLB son, he had already had another-- another degree two years previously, which was BA Economics. You see? And now they are in Johannesburg, both of them. And-- and they've got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris, the eldest, has got three children, which are a boy and two girls. One-- the name of the boy is Doron. And the girls, the eldest is Aviva. And the other one is Shira Hanan. And Aviva, as a matter of fact, she had the bat mitzvah last week. That's why we are here now, holidaying with my husband. And my other son, Ronnie--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wife of Morris is Wendy. And my other son are named Aaron and Ronald, which he has a son and a daughter. The son name is Leon. Like, because the Sephardic, we named the-- the first born after the father, which Leon, Leon. And his wife is also Wendy. So now we get confusion to distinguish the one Wendy to another Wendy, when we want to speak about them. Anyhow, and also, the bar mitzvah of my youngest son was also the week-- right before this week. And we wait-- we came here for simchas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think that is God will. I've been telling all the time that because there you don't have to be strong, you don't have to be clever. You don't have to be-- you know, you don't have to-- you don't have to be qualification to survive. Because any day that passes there, you know, it was like 10 years in a usual life of a person. And as I can tell you, you know, as I can say to you, just up to now, is this thing's happening 50 years ago. But if you ask me in 50 years-- if I'm alive, of course not-- but I will tell you exactly how it happened. Maybe I'll forget what happened in 1960 or 1970. But that year that I spent in the concentration camp is carved in my heart, in my-- in my body, in my-- in my thoughts, everything. Day by day, I can describe what's happened in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there been any decisions that you've made in your life, or things that you've done that were influenced by your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, whatever-- you know, if-- if I-- whatever, you know, you have to live today. Because if you take something, for instance, oh, I have to do something. I will do next month. I would say, you don't know what's happening next month. Whatever you have to do today and you can do, and you're in good health, and if you want to do anything, you can do it what-- while you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do yo want to tell your story? Why did you agree to do this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I want to tell my story. Because it's-- to my-- to my mind, it's impossible that the human being could do things like that that happened, what they was doing in-- in another-- in-- to another person. How can-- you know, you can say it's impossible, the things-- Even myself, when I tell my stories to myself in bed, and many times, you know, many nights, even after so many years, many nights. I don't say every night, but many nights I dream that the Germans are after me, following me, they want to kill me. They want to give death. You know that they are still-- Even today, I don't believe myself, those things, they were true. Sometimes, say maybe they're all imagination. Because you cannot believe it, you know, the things a man-- a human being can do that to another human being, impossible. Even today, you see in the street a dog that is dying, you go and try to help a dog, an animal, a cat, even-- you know, any single-- any single thing, you know, a-- a person, or even a-- a soul that is suffering, you must try to-- to help. And the thing is I want to say to the world so they know, because nobody believe that exist even today, you know, in this era, in time, many people, they think there is-- the Holocaust is invention of the-- of the-- of the-- some Jewish. They think this doesn't exist. Because let me tell you, last year it was the-- it was the-- premiere, the premiere of the Spielberg's 'Schindler's List,' which of course, many, you know-- which, of course, I went to see. Many of our crowd, they don't want to see, anymore, concentration camp. Because as I tell you, they also start to-- reminiscing, you know, about all the things. And they get, again, you know, depressed and all this. But myself, I say, no. I have to know everything, whatever it's-- somebody has to give details from that camps, from that thing. I want to know about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to see that-- that-- that 'Schindler's List.' Though it was a beautiful film and everything, but still, you know, still you cannot describe the horror as it was true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you know, this camp, where they stay in Schindler's place, it was, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like a paradise. I wish I was-- when I was deported, I was in-- in Schindler's camp. But we were in the horror even worse than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while I was-- telling you, I was to this premiere, I came out. And I heard two Christian woman saying one to another one-- not myself. It was the-- the-- the lady chairman of the [INAUDIBLE], of the-- of society. And they were saying to one another, you think it's true, what happened? What we saw there, what we saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one say, I don't think so. I think it must be more propaganda. And then, this lady, who I happened to be behind them. And I say, excuse me, ladies. That is not propaganda. If you want, there-- the lady-- you see that lady, you know, was walking. That is a survivor. She can tell you in person if it is propaganda. She called me and said, these ladies, and they think it's propaganda. They feel so embarrassed, they run away, the two of them, the two ladies. They didn't want to hear anymore. Because they thought-- they thought it doesn't exist. Even today, they don't believe. Because it's-- it's impossible, they say. Anybody, you know, who has got-- even you're now, you know, you are cruel. But still, you cannot do to other people what the Germans were doing to the Jewish people. Impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your final message to your children, grandchildren, to the world, or anyone who watches?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean to say, the-- the worst-- the message is not to do evil things. Not to start any war. Because once you start it, you cannot stop. It's a machinery, it goes to [? worst. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: To tell you an example, you know, I mean, it's not good. Even somebody has done to you, like you say, bad, you must not say two wrong makes a right, you know? You-- I-- I think you must always strong, you know, show compassion. Never mind what-- I'm this-- I never, for instance, go to bed and I know I done wrong to some person. In my mind, I feel I am guilty. I have to do something about it, to fix these things, you know? That is the-- the main thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want the world to be like-- like-- like we're friendly, one another. That is what the Jewish people, we wait, the Messiah. When the Messiah will arrive, it will mean everybody will be friends, one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there won't be anymore evil things, going one to another one. You know? That is what that I think. You know? To be-- it will be so nice if the world were all peaceful and nice together. Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Isaac Hougnou. He was a tailor in Rhodes Island. He was a wonderful and-- and clever man. He was so nice-looking and-- and-- and young looking that when we arrive in the concentration camp, you know, they-- they mistaken him for-- they mistook him for a young person. And he was sent to-- together with the youngs, to in the labor camps, instead of be sent with his wife, my mother, to the-- to the chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This taken was-- this picture was taken when my father was about 30, or 28. 28, 30. And he was-- actually, that picture, he was very, very big-- its in very enlargement. And he was standing, you know, in the-- in our lounge. And it was a beautiful picture. You know, we had it there. And then after the war, you know, while we were in-- in Bologna, one of our friends left to go to Rhodes Island to see if she can find any-- any-- any belongings. Because she was a rich girl, she can find any belongings still in Rhodes Island, that she can get it. We also would have the opportunity to go to-- to-- to get to Rhodes Island. We could have offered-- you know, we got offered a free ticket and everything. But we didn't want to go because it was too soon after the concentration camp. And we were not interested in belongings or possessions or-- or money. But we were very sad. And we thought if we went to Rhodes Island, maybe we were going to suffer even more, you know, than not going. So we decide not to go. My friend, she went. And then she say, she went to my house. She went to my house to see if she can find anything, not of value, of sentimental value to bring us home. Because she knew that valuables, she will never find. That-- that whatever there was, you know, it was already taken by us to the Germans. And the other thing, the community of Rhodes Island, you know, what it was form after the concentration camp and after the war, they collected the-- they went from house to house. And they put all the valuables in boxes and tins, to-- you know, so if somebody comes and asks for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any case, while she was visiting my house, around-- it was quite a, you know, a big house-- she went into the kitchen. At the time in our place, you know, we didn't had-- we didn't have a electric stove. But it was stoves made that-- that we can put the coal, you know? Coal-- coal-- coal stoves.

speaker: interviewee
text: While she was looking, just she opened the coal stove. And she find that inside of the-- amongst the coal, you know, there was that big picture of my father, that it was all the time with the lovely-- lovely frame. It was there in two pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you can see, by the picture is cutted in the middle in two pieces, lying there. And then my friend say, can I have this picture? Because I want to give to the daughters that still live in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she brought it to Italy and give it to us, that big picture. So at the time, when we were in Italy, as I say, we were-- there not money, enough money for my sister and myself to make it, you know, the same size of the picture that it was in Rhodes Island. And we never thought of making one for each survivor, you know, survivor like myself, my sister, myself, my two brothers in Africa. We just made two copy only to give to the brothers. And I mean, [? this ?] [? is ?] souvenir. One for the one in-- you know, the one-- the two lives in the town. They were living in the Belgian Congo. And we-- we give them to them. So now, one of the brothers died after a few years while we were in the Congo. He had emigrated with his family to Capetown. He died in Capetown. And the wife say, I think you shall have that picture that you know, you wanted. And then the other brother took the other picture with him when he went to live in South America, where he lived for a few years. But now, he's also die from natural causes, in Montevideo. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my brother Aaron, who died in the concentration camp in Auschwitz. This picture was taken by the-- the two girls are survivors of the concentration camp. And they were his friends. Which that picture was sent to the friend's brother in France, before the war. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my sister, Diamonte, who was with me in the concentration camp all the time. Now she lives in Capetown. And in the photograph, she is with the son of  my late brother, Joseph. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my brother, Perahia, who brought us to Africa. And after a few years when we were in Africa, he emigrated to South America. Exactly to Montevideo, where he married and he had a son. But now he died from natural causes. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] You can see what-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken on the 23 of-- of of March, at the bar mitzvah of my eldest grandson, Leon, in Johannesburg. And they are-- the first is my husband. And then is my two sons, Morris, standing in the back, and Ronnie, between myself and his wife. And there are the two wives. That is Wendy, the wife of Morris. And the other one is-- the one in red is the wife of Ronnie. And in front are my five grandchildren. The first one is Shira. On the left is the-- the youngest son of my son, Morris. And then there is Aviva, the eldest son of my son, Morris. And then it's Leon, the eldest son of my son, Ronnie. And then is Doron, the-- the only son of my son, Morris. And Tessa, the daughter of my son, Ronnie, and Wendy. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is Wendy, Morris' wife. This is my eldest son, Morris. This is my husband, Leon. This is myself. [LAUGHTER] This-- this is Ronnie. And this is his wife, Wendy. And in the front are my clever grandson, Doron, my beautiful granddaughter, the eldest granddaughter, Aviva, which she had bat mitzvah two weeks ago. And then there's my very clever Leon, which is my grandson, my eldest grandson. And he had the bar mitzvah last week. And he did very well. And that is my younger granddaughter, Shira. And that is my lovely and beautiful Tessa, the daughter of Ronnie and Wendy. So we are really very happy to be together in that good occasion. Because we are here for the bar mitzvah and bat mitzvah at the same times. And we are very pleased to be together tonight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] RONNIE (SON): Oh, [? what's ?] [? this? ?] So what else can you do? You hair? What about your hair? No. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: RONNIE (SON): He's looking at you. Just do a close-up of the grandkids. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] And I must thank my husband, Leon, for being such a wonderful husband for 42 years. [LAUGHTER] And I wish my children, they will reach also, their wedding anniversary, to be old and happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHTER] AVIVA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Don't. [LAUGHTER] Come on. [LAUGHTER] WENDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Shh, shh. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Everybody's all looking good. ?] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Done.
